retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Osteoclasts -- Clinical Significance -- Periodontitis
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology of periodontitis is that as bacterial cells increase in number, they ultimately attract immune cells such as macrophages. Those immune cells release inflammatory cytokines such as IL-6, which increases the migration of osteoclasts and results in increased bone resorption of the alveolar bone of teeth, destroying the insertion sites of teeth. [11] Additionally, T-lymphocytes can be called on to help destroy the bacterial infection, and they have RANKL, which can directly stimulate osteoclastogenesis and cause bone resorption of alveolar bone. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Gradually the host as a response to the bacterial infection by classic innate immune response. [24] The response includes signs of acute inflammation including increased redness of gingiva, bleeding and swollen gums as well migration of neutrophils to the site of inflammation. The innate immunity also activates the primary host cells of the body, preparing the body for defense against bacterial infection as well as triggers the adaptive immunity. [24] The Innate immunity also triggers the host cell to differentiate into more specific cells, in turn, increasing the pro-inflammatory mediators like Interleukin 1-beta, prostaglandins and tumor necrosis factor. [24] The cascade gets activated resulting in adaptive immunity getting into pace by activating specific T and B lymphocytes. There is documentation of the role of B and T cell involvement with activation of RANK (Receptor activator of nuclear factor-kB) which results in bone loss due to the activation of osteoclasts. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Gingivitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: There is increased collagenolytic activity in this stage along with a rise in the number of macrophages, plasma cells, and T and B lymphocytes. However, the predominant cells are plasma cells and B lymphocytes. In this stage, a small gingival pocket lined with a pocket epithelium is created. The lesion exhibits a high degree of organization. It has been suggested that the severity of gingivitis correlates with a growth in the B cells and plasma cells population, and a decrease in the number of T cells. An established lesion may follow 2 paths: it can either remain stable for months or years, or progress to a more destructive lesion, which appears to be related to a change in the microbial flora or infection of the gingiva. This stage is reversible following effective periodontal therapy, which increases the number of microorganisms associated with periodontal health and correlates with a reduction in plasma cells and lymphocytes. [12] 4. Advanced lesion

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Periodontium -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Gingivitis is an initial response to combat the first insult to the periodontium. As the name suggests, it is an inflammation of the gingiva, a component of the periodontium resulting from the accumulation of microbial plaque near the gingival sulcus. Gingivitis has a delayed appearance in children with a predominance of T lymphocyte infiltrates. [10] Most cases are transient, non-progressive, and reversible. However, it can progress to Periodontitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Disease -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Page and Schroeder were the first to describe the histopathology of periodontal disease, identifying 4 distinct stages based on clinical and histological tissue changes. These stages—initial, early, established, and advanced—illustrate the progressive nature of the disease, beginning with subclinical inflammation and potentially leading to irreversible tissue destruction and bone loss. Initial lesion: The initial lesion is characterized by plaque accumulation, which leads to vascular changes and intercellular gap formation, thereby increasing the flow of gingival crevicular fluid. Adhesion molecules attract polymorphonuclear neutrophils (PMNs) to the lesion, while T lymphocytes modulate fibroblast activity in the affected area. Clinically, this stage is asymptomatic.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Periodontium -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Initially, the gingival crevicular fluid amount is increased due to vascular changes in response to the initial insult. At this benign stage, polymorphonuclear neutrophils are attracted to the lesion site, and T lymphocytes are responsible for fibroblasts. The early lesion is characterized by redness. At this stage, PMNs clear and breakdown the collagen fibers, which increases the previously made space for infiltrates. At this stage, the established lesion is dominated by B cells and leukocyte aggregation. This initiates the lesion side transformation by changing the junctional and sulcular epithelium into an extremely vulnerable epithelium called the pocket epithelium. This is apparent as bleeding upon gentle gingival manipulation. An advanced lesion characterized as loss of gingival fibers and alveolar bone is caused by the migration of biofilm into the pocket and creating an environment for anaerobic bacterias' proliferation. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: As previously stated, the host response includes various cells, mediators, effector molecules, and antibodies. The cellular elements include polymorphonuclear leukocytes (PMN), lymphocytes, monocytes/macrophages, and plasma cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Lichen Planus -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The antigen-specific mechanism involves triggering events that expose OLP-specific antigens, leading to activation and recruitment of CD4+ helper T cells and subsequent release of pro-inflammatory T-helper 1 cytokines, including tumor necrosis factor α and interferon γ. [20] CD8+ T cell-mediated cytotoxicity targets the basal cell layer of the epithelium, resulting in basement membrane disruption, T-cell migration into the epithelium, and keratinocyte apoptosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Psoriatic Arthritis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: CD8+ T cells, integral to psoriatic arthritis, are substantiated by their connection with HLA Class I alleles, the oligoclonal expansion of CD8+ T cells, and their association with late-stage HIV infection. [27] Other immune cells involved in the pathogenesis include CD4+ T helper 17 cells that produce IL-17 and IL-22; type 3 innate lymphoid cells that produce IL-17 and IL-22; and gamma-delta T cells that produce IL-17 and TNF-alpha. [27] [26] These proinflammatory cytokines recruit neutrophils that enter the synovial fluid, activate synoviocytes, promote angiogenesis locally, and activate osteoclasts, resulting in bone destruction and osteoblasts that promote new bone formation. [26] [31] [27] [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Osteoclasts -- Pathophysiology -- T-Cell Mediated Generation of Osteoclasts
retrieved_docs.content: Similar to osteoblast expression of RANKL, arguably the most important osteoclast determining factor, activated T-cells also express RANKL. When there is a persistence of elevated active T-cells, it can lead to increased osteoclast differentiation, and ultimately, excess bone resorption.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: To understand the pathophysiology of periodontal disease, it is essential to know about the complex dental biofilm as well as the Immune response associated with the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Polymorphonuclear leukocytes play a significant role in the host response, as they secrete cytoplastic granules with enzymatic properties. Although the primary responsibility of PMNs is to protect the host, they can cause structural damage to tissue cells and extracellular matrices. [11] Thus, we can see how the accumulation of PMNs plays a significant role in the destruction of periapical tissues during the acute phase of AP. [7] [11] Other host cells that play crucial roles are lymphocytes, macrophages, and osteoclasts.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- History and Physical -- Chronic Apical Periodontitis
retrieved_docs.content: In the chronic phase, activated T- cells produce a variety of cytokines that down-regulate and suppress the osteoclastic activity (reducing bone resorption) and increase the production of connective tissue growth factor (TGF-beta). [7] The reduction in bone resorption and the regeneration of connective tissue structure explains why chronic lesions can remain dormant and asymptomatic for extended periods without significant radiographic changes. Histopathologically, chronic apical periodontitis lesions are encapsulated with fibrous tissue, and some may be epithelized. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Bisphosphonate-Related Jaw Osteonecrosis -- Pathophysiology -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: BPs inhibit the proliferation and viability of oral keratinocytes, damaging the oral mucosa integrity and increasing the risk of infection. [23] [24] [25] Also, BPs impair the immune response to infection by activating gamma, delta T-cells stimulating the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and later depletion of T cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Necrotizing Periodontal Diseases -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Although NPDs are infectious, immunosuppression is the most significant predisposing factor. Cases of necrotizing gingivitis and necrotizing periodontitis occur almost exclusively in individuals with immunodeficiency, most notably those with HIV infection. A CD4 count below 200 in an individual with HIV is more strongly associated with necrotizing periodontitis than any other known risk factor. [5] Additional conditions such as leukemia, neutropenia, diabetes mellitus, and prolonged immunosuppressive therapy have also been implicated. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: CD4 helper T cells: Facilitate cell-to-cell interactions and cytokine release to activate and control immune and inflammatory responses

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1459
retrieved_docs.content: Microbiology is identified as an applicable qualifier for the oral conditions قيلة مثانية, قيلة مستقيمية, and اضطراب اللسان, indicating that microbiological factors are relevant in the assessment or management of these disorders. Bacterial vaccines are, in turn, an applicable qualifier for microbiology, reflecting the role of immunization strategies within the broader microbiological context. The term “Without” is noted as not being associated with either قيلة مثانية or قيلة مستقيمية, suggesting that the absence of a particular factor does not have a direct relationship with these specific conditions. Together, these connections highlight the importance of microbiological considerations and vaccine‑related interventions in certain oral pathologies, while clarifying that “Without” does not contribute to their etiology or presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2721
retrieved_docs.content: Amphotericin B is identified as a therapeutic agent that may be used to treat diseases of white blood cells, indicating its potential role in managing conditions that affect the immune cell population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 751
retrieved_docs.content: Crizanlizumab is identified as a selective adhesion molecule inhibitor, placing it within the product class of selective adhesion molecule inhibitors. In addition, crizanlizumab is classified as both an immunomodulatory agent (imunomodulátory) and an immunosuppressive agent (imunosupresiva), reflecting its dual capacity to modify and dampen immune system activity. These properties align crizanlizumab with the broader disposition of immunomodulators, indicating that its therapeutic action involves regulation of immune responses through targeted inhibition of adhesion molecules. This integrated classification underscores crizanlizumab’s role in modulating immune pathways and its potential utility in conditions where controlling immune cell adhesion and activity is clinically beneficial.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 245
retrieved_docs.content: shared ATC classification, while also possessing distinct mechanistic attributes such as inhibition of selective adhesion molecules or modulation of immune responses. Recognizing both the common therapeutic grouping and the unique pharmacologic actions of each agent supports more integrated patient care and informs clinicians when selecting or comparing treatments within the hematology and immunology domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1600
retrieved_docs.content: Indomethacin is identified as the parent compound for the immunomodulatory drug class, meaning that the immunomodulatory agents have indomethacin as their “has parent” relationship. This connection indicates that immunomodulatory drugs are derived from or chemically related to indomethacin, highlighting a pharmacological lineage that can inform understanding of their mechanisms and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2184
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease includes human vaccination as a subordinate concept, indicating that human vaccination is regarded as a specific form of immunization employed to prevent or modulate autoimmune disease in humans.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2276
retrieved_docs.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية) encompasses several distinct conditions, including التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). Each of these diseases is characterized by an inappropriate immune response that targets the body’s own tissues, leading to joint inflammation, destruction of red blood cells, or inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities share a common autoimmune basis aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and applying immunomodulatory therapies across this spectrum of immune‑mediated illnesses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1602
retrieved_docs.content: Crizanlizumab is classified within the therapeutic class of Immunological Agents [TC] and is designated as an Immunomodulator (disposition). It is a specific type of Selective adhesion molecule inhibitor (product), reflecting its mechanism of action that targets adhesion molecules to modulate immune cell interactions. Additionally, crizanlizumab is recognized as an imunosupresiva, indicating its broader role as an immunosuppressive agent. Together, these classifications emphasize crizanlizumab’s function as an immunological drug that modulates immune responses through selective inhibition of adhesion molecules, positioning it within both the immunomodulator and immunosuppressive categories of therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1762
retrieved_docs.content: Deflazacort is categorized under the therapeutic class Immunological Agents [TC], indicating that it functions as an immunomodulatory medication used to suppress immune activity in various clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylmuramyl-alanyl-isoglutamin is classified as an immunostimulant, meaning it belongs to the group of agents that activate or enhance the body’s immune response. As an immunostimulant, acetylmuramyl-alanyl-isoglutamin can be used to boost immune function in various clinical contexts, supporting the body’s ability to recognize and combat pathogens or abnormal cells.
